title: Medicare Home Health Services
sectionTitle: Medicare home health eligibility and covered services
content: Medicare home health services may help stroke survivors who recently left the hospital and need skilled care at home. Coverage may include part-time or intermittent skilled nursing care, physical therapy, occupational therapy, speech-language pathology, medical social services, some home health aide services, durable medical equipment, and medical supplies when program conditions are met. Families should ask the discharge planner or clinician whether home health is medically appropriate and whether the person meets Medicare homebound and skilled-care conditions.

title: Medicare Home-Based Rehabilitation Services
sectionTitle: PT, OT, and speech therapy through home health
content: For stroke recovery after discharge, home health may include physical therapy, occupational therapy, and speech-language pathology when the person meets Medicare home health conditions. CareBridge should not automatically claim that the person qualifies. A safer next step is to recommend asking the provider, discharge planner, or home health agency whether home-based PT, OT, or speech therapy is appropriate for the survivor's condition.

title: Medicare Ambulance Services
content: Medicare Part B may cover ambulance transportation when travel in another vehicle could endanger the person's health and the trip is medically necessary. Some non-emergency ambulance transportation may require documentation, such as a written order and evidence of medical necessity. Families should ask the clinician whether non-emergency ambulance documentation is appropriate instead of assuming general transportation is covered.

title: Medicare Long-Term Care Coverage Limits
sectionTitle: Medicare and custodial long-term care expectations
content: Families may assume Medicare pays for ongoing custodial long-term care after a stroke, but Medicare generally does not cover most long-term custodial care. CareBridge should explain this boundary early and suggest exploring Medicaid, Home- and Community-Based Services waivers, state aging and disability offices, or other local support pathways when the family needs ongoing in-home help.

title: American Stroke Association Family Caregivers
sectionTitle: Caregiver education and long-term support after stroke
content: The American Stroke Association describes family caregivers as an important source of long-term support for stroke recovery and rehabilitation. Caregiver resources can help families understand caregiver well-being, practical care needs, finances after stroke, fall prevention, support groups, and educational guidance. CareBridge should surface caregiver support when caregiver burden is high or when family members ask for practical caregiving help.

title: American Stroke Association Resources for Caregivers, Family and Friends
sectionTitle: Stroke Family Warmline and human guidance
content: The American Stroke Association offers resources for stroke survivors, caregivers, and family members, including access to trained stroke specialists through the Stroke Family Warmline. CareBridge should recommend this type of resource when a family feels overwhelmed, needs human guidance, or is unsure where to start after discharge.

title: American Stroke Association Resources for Stroke Survivors
sectionTitle: State aging, disability, HCBS, and community support pathways
content: Stroke survivors and caregivers may need community-based support after discharge. The American Stroke Association points families toward state aging and disability offices, local referrals, support groups, Medicaid Home- and Community-Based Services pathways when applicable, and other state-level resources. CareBridge should ask for the user's state and Medicaid status before suggesting state-specific HCBS or local aging and disability resources.

title: Eldercare Locator
sectionTitle: Local aging and disability service navigation
content: The Eldercare Locator connects older adults and caregivers with local aging services and support resources. For stroke survivors and caregivers, this can be useful when the family needs local referrals for respite, meals, transportation, in-home help, caregiver support, or aging and disability services. CareBridge should recommend this when local service navigation is needed and ask for the user's location.

title: Family Caregiver Alliance
sectionTitle: State-by-state caregiver education and service discovery
content: Family Caregiver Alliance provides caregiver education, support resources, and state-by-state navigation through tools such as the Family Care Navigator. This is not a guaranteed benefit, but it can help caregivers discover services and learn how to manage caregiving responsibilities. CareBridge should surface this when a caregiver needs education, state-specific resource discovery, or support planning.

title: Caregiver Action Network
content: Caregiver Action Network provides practical education, caregiver tools, and support resources for family caregivers. The resource is broadly available and does not function like a formal eligibility-based benefit. CareBridge should recommend caregiver education resources when a caregiver reports stress, confusion, or uncertainty about hands-on care.

title: Medicaid Home- and Community-Based Services Waiver Pathways
sectionTitle: State-specific HCBS eligibility and in-home support
content: Medicaid Home- and Community-Based Services pathways may help some people receive services in the community instead of institutional care, but eligibility and available services vary by state. For stroke survivors who may qualify for Medicaid and need ongoing in-home or community-based support, CareBridge should ask for Medicaid status and state, then direct the family to the state Medicaid or Human Services agency for state-specific screening.

title: Vocational Rehabilitation Programs
sectionTitle: Return-to-work and vocational rehabilitation after stroke
content: Vocational rehabilitation programs may support people with disabilities who have work-related recovery goals. Services can vary by state and may include counseling, job training, medical or psychological services related to employment, and individualized employment support. CareBridge should surface this pathway for younger stroke survivors or users who ask about returning to work.

title: Get Paid as a Caregiver for a Family Member
sectionTitle: Family caregiver payment pathways through state programs
content: Some state Medicaid programs may allow a family member or friend to be paid for caregiving when the person receiving care has a disability and qualifies for the relevant state program. Rules, eligibility, and payment vary by state. CareBridge should avoid promising payment and instead recommend checking the user's state Medicaid office or disability services pathway.

title: Texas Medicaid Non-Emergency Medical Transportation
content: Texas Medicaid's Medical Transportation Program is an example of state-specific non-emergency medical transportation support for eligible Medicaid clients who do not have another way to get to covered health care services. CareBridge can use this as a transportation assistance example, but should clearly mark it as Texas-specific and ask the user's state before recommending it.
